retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Gutta-Percha
retrieved_docs.content: Gutta-percha has many advantages, such as ease of manipulation, radiopacity, low toxicity, and plasticity. However, it does not bond to dentin and shrinks when cooled. [5] [25] Gutta-percha cones are available in standardized 0.02 taper as specified by the International Organization of Standardization and non-standardized sizes. [5] This allows clinicians to mechanically prepare the canal space using various commercial rotary systems using standardized file sizes. When the canal is prepared to a given file size, a gutta-percha cone of the same size can be inserted and adapted to the canal. [1] [5] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- Gutta Percha
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: Ease of manipulation Radiopaque Low toxicity and plasticity Relatively inexpensive [1] [5] [25] Disadvantages: No dentin-bonding properties Material shrinkage upon setting [1] [5] [25] Bioceramic Core Obturation Materials Advantages: Biocompatible Ability to promote biomineralization High fracture resistance Antimicrobial Dimensional stability upon setting [26] [27] Disadvantages: Technically difficult to manipulate Expensive Shorter shelf-life (cv other core materials) [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Colestipol -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical trial evaluated colestipol's viability as an oral phosphate binder in hemodialysis patients and reported positive outcomes. The findings suggest the potential for a larger-scale trial to ascertain colestipol's effectiveness as a phosphate binder. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- Calcium Hydroxide Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages Antimicrobial properties Milder cytotoxicity Promotes tissue healing [13] Disadvantages Solubility

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Toxicodendron Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicodendron dermatitis is typically self-limited and resolves within a couple of weeks. [1] Initial management consists of immediate irrigation with soap and decontamination of clothing upon exposure. [4] Specific instruction should be given to clean under fingernails as they are commonly missed. [21] Mild detergents are a reasonable choice when compared to expensive alternatives. [22] Additional anecdotal options include cool, moist compresses, oatmeal baths, calamine lotion, and topical astringents. [7] [23] A small study comparing topical commercially available cream containing polyethylene granules, sodium lauroyl sarcosinate, nonoxynol-9, C12-15 pareth-9, disodium EDTA, quaternium-15, carbomer (2%), triethanolamine, and water, to placebo showed improvement in symptoms if applied early. Some literature suggests the use of prophylactic homeopathic oral preparations of poison ivy may be somewhat protective, but randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are lacking. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Gutta-Percha
retrieved_docs.content: Gutta-percha is the most commonly used core obturation material in modern endodontics. [25] They are manufactured as cones comprised mostly of zinc oxide and gutta-percha with added plasticizers and radiopacifiers. [5] Gutta-percha is a naturally occurring thermoplastic material that originates from specific trees in the Malaysian region. [25] The popularity of gutta-percha as an obturation material stems from its variable chemical composition. Gutta-percha exists in 2 crystalline phases, alpha and beta, and can cycle between these phases when heated is applied or removed. The alpha phase of gutta-percha occurs when heated, producing a tacky material that can flow under pressure. Alternatively, the beta phase is when the gutta-percha is unheated, forming a solid mass of compactable material. [1] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Docusate -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Docusate has a well-documented safety and tolerability profile and has been in use in the United States since the 1950s. Animal studies have demonstrated that docusate sodium ear drops can cause significant ototoxicity. Therefore, docusate ear drops should be avoided in patients with tympanic membrane perforation. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Polyethylene Glycol -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group study assessed the safety of isosmotic polyethylene glycol electrolyte balanced solution (PMF-100) over six months in N=70 patients with chronic constipation. The study found that over 20 weeks, the dosage decreased progressively, and there was a 77% complete reduction of constipation symptoms in PMF-100 versus 20% in the placebo-controlled group. [4] Furthermore, there were no significant differences in adverse events between the two groups.

retrieved_docs.title: OSHA Formaldehyde Safety -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorimetry utilizes gaseous formaldehyde trapped in an aqueous solution within a stripping coil, allowing the measurement of formaldehyde levels between 0.001 to 3 ppm every 2 seconds to 2 minutes.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholestyramine Resin  -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Constipation: preventable with adequate water and fiber intake

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Root Canal Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: The role of the sealer is critical for establishing a fluid-tight seal of the root canal as it fills the vacant space between the prepared dentinal wall and the core filling material. [1] [5] It also reaches lateral canals and tubules inaccessible to gutta-percha cones and provides antimicrobial action. [5] Most sealers are toxic before setting, so care should be taken to avoid the extrusion of sealers into the periapical tissues. [6] Sealers induce different levels of periapical inflammation; however, this effect is usually transient and does not interfere with tissue healing. [7] Sealers are partly reabsorbed after exposure to tissue fluids. The core material should occupy almost all of the canal, and sealers should only be applied in small quantities. Several types of sealers are available, which vary in formulation and properties. They are classified into 5 main groups according to their composition: zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE) based, calcium hydroxide based, glass ionomer-based, resin-based, and bioceramic sealers. The properties of an ideal sealer have been outlined in the literature. It is important to note that no material has been proven to eliminate leakage nor fulfill all the ideals.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Root Canal Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Several techniques are used to insert the sealer into the root canal and ensure it evenly coats the prepared surface. One technique utilizes a screw-like file that attaches to a slow-speed handpiece called lentulo spiral. [36] The lentulo spiral is dipped into the sealer and inserted into the root canal. When the handpiece is activated, the lentulo spiral spins and evenly distributes the sealer on the walls through centrifugal force. [37] Its use has several downsides, including the risk of instrument fracture due to the instrument being very fragile and easily breakable. [38] [36] Additionally, it has been shown to accelerate the setting of resin-based sealers due to the stirring effect. [19] Other popular techniques for inserting sealer include the use of gutta-percha cones. The operator butters the pre-fit paper point or gutta-percha cone in the sealer and then inserts it in the canal using a pumping action to ensure even coating. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Root Canal Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Properties of an ideal sealer material: Ability to establish a fluid-tight seal Radiopaque (to be visible on radiographs) Antimicrobial Non-shrinking upon setting Non-staining Non-irritating to periapical tissues Insoluble in tissue fluid Biocompatible [1] [8]

Its chelating action stabilizes the drug solutions, extending shelf‑life and enhancing safety. All listed agents share the pharmacologic class of opioid analgesics, acting on opioid receptors to provide potent relief of moderate to severe pain. The diversity of active ingredients, concentration strengths and dosage forms—ranging from injectable solutions and cartridges to oral solutions and combination capsules—allows clinicians to individualize pain‑management strategies while recognizing class‑wide properties and the common excipient‑related considerations associated with EDTA‑disodium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2733
retrieved_docs.content: Benzyl alcohol serves as an inactive ingredient in multiple pharmaceutical products. It is incorporated into sodium tetradecyl sulfate 30 MG/ML injectable solution, where it functions as a preservative or solubilizing agent. Benzyl alcohol is also present as an inactive component of capsaicin 1 MG/ML topical cream formulations, including the generic capsaicin 1 MG/ML topical cream and the branded product Capzasin‑HP. These uses illustrate benzyl alcohol’s role in both injectable and topical dermatologic preparations, contributing to product stability and patient safety.
